title: 2025/12/03 - SSC (Part 3)
displayTitle: 2025-12-03 - Social Services and Community Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Housing and Urban Development [Part 3]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Housing and Urban Development
description: Wednesday 3 December 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Housing and Urban Development Hearing of evidence: Ministry for Housing and Urban Development Brad Ward, Acting Chief Executive Jessica Smith, Deputy Chief Executive, Māori-Crown Relations Jessica Garland, Programmes and Contracts
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Housing and Urban Development. Ministry for Housing and Urban Development. Brad Ward, Acting Chief Executive

chunks.text: Well, good afternoon and welcome to the Social (door shuts) Housing and Communities Select Committee. Um, thank you for being here this afternoon. So, what I might just do is get you to introduce yourselves, um, and then make some opening comments. And then, from there, we'll move to questions from members. Great. Um, thank you. Um, good afternoon. Um, thanks for the opportunity to talk about our important work. Um, just introducing colleagues, so to my right, we've got Jess Smith, uh, DC in Maori housing area, Jessica Garland,

uh, GM in our delivery area. And then, uh, some of the... well, a lot of the team from HUD behind me. I won't go into names. Um, look, just in terms of opening comments, the challenges we face around, um, housing supply affordability and access have developed across, uh, many decades and require a concerted and aligned effort to resolve.

So we'll all be aware, New Zealand does... has not built enough houses. Uh, people in lower incomes have fewer housing choices. And the funding system for housing and urban development has been, complicated, confusing, and investment hasn't always been focused on value for money. Uh, HUD manages over 8.4 billion in funding per year. Uh, over the past year, we've focused on four key areas, uh, to help improve housing outcomes for New Zealanders. First being enabling efficient land markets, and that's really the Going For Housing growth program, um, and obviously working

very closely with MfE and other agencies on that. Delivering other housing s- be- better housing support to those who need it. Um, and the most recent example of that is the investment plan that was launched two weeks ago. Uh, thirdly, addressing residual barriers to urban development. Big focus, uh, there on, um, helping where we can around city and regional deals. There's obviously three in the co- under in discussion at the moment. Um, and also, uh, for example, the City Rail Link in Auckland. Uh, and the fourth is protecting rights and obligations through efficient

regulations. Um, and case t- uh, two recent cases in point there are obviously, uh, the work around, um, Retirement Villages Act, uh, which I believe announcements are due soon, um, and property managers as well. And obviously, we had j- on Monday, the, uh, PET Bonds picnic, um, which was a really good, uh, success as well and will make a difference along the lives of renters. Um, that is it from me, so happy to answer questions.

All right. Thank you. First question from Kieran. Thank you. Thanks very much for coming in. Um, I understand that the latest homelessness insights briefing is due this month. Has a draft been provided to the Minister? So, uh, yep. Uh, the, the draft has been provided to Minister. Yep.

And so, conscious that, uh, we will need to wait till that comes out, uh, but in your ongoing conversations with frontline providers, have they indicated a, an additional increase in homelessness from what they can see? So we're in regular d- uh, discussions with frontline providers and councils and other, and other parties.

Um, the report, when it's, uh, released, and that is, I think as Minister Bishop said, will be released shortly. Um, that will have some further insights, um, uh, in that report. Um, I don't wanna get ahead of exactly what's in the report because, um, it is with ministers. They have, uh, only got it recently and I think it's fair, uh, that they have time to review it.

What I can say is that, um, as ministers previously said, um, we recognize that the challenges around homelessness and wanting to make an impact and wanting to make a dent in that, um, issue. Um, and so we are very focused at the moment, for example, on making real headway around our September announcements, which is getting those Housing First places, um, uh, settled, uh, with providers and tenants, um, and also working on some of the other initiatives that we've got in place. For example, improving the utilization of transitional

housing so more homeless people can get into, uh, transitional housing quicker as well. So you are, uh, have ongoing, uh, engagement with frontline providers, not solely for the purpose of compiling that report. Um, you've previously indicated in, um, uh, appearings, appearances before this committee that, uh, given the census is the, the most robust, I think is the term you used- Mm-hmm.

... for measuring levels of homelessness, you're reliant on feedback from frontline providers in between those times. So in that engagement with frontline providers, have they indicated another increase in homelessness? So I, as I said just before, I'll let the, um, when the insights report come out, comes out, it will provide, um, more information.

And there's a range of sources of information from, um, lists and counts from homeless providers, uh, to, um, councils and also reports from public. Um, what I can say is, is obviously there are, you know, uh, reports from some providers, like take, um, Auckland City Mission, um, around the number of homeless in Auckland.

Uh, we are aware of that. Um, but I am very much, and the team at HUD, working very closely with MSD and the providers, are very much focused on getting on with what we said in September, which was getting those Housing First places tenanted, getting the other services, support services, uh, underway.

And we've made really good s- uh, headway there. And also very much focused on getting TH utilization, uh, improved and very much focused on getting social housing into those areas where it is, uh, most needed as well. Okay. Well, um, I didn't ask the content of the report. I asked about your ongoing engagement with frontline providers, but I don't think I'm gonna get any further with that.

So I'll ask specifically...... um, around the 300 additional Housing First places that were announced in August. How many people are now in a placement as a result of that announcement? So, the latest figures I have, so of the 300, uh, which was one part of the announcement, uh, we have in, uh- (coughs) Yeah, 90 places at least, and 44 households have been housed to date.

So 44 out of the 300? That's right. Okay. Thank you. Mm-hmm. Uh, supplementary, Tamatha. Um, just, uh, ̌kau tāpāi. Just on the, um, questions around census and data. Mm-hmm. Um, could you talk to us a little bit about, uh, how you're seeing, uh, the experience of women in terms of growing homelessness and rough sleeping, and what trends you're seeing when it comes to women experiencing homelessness or rough sleeping?

So, so whai- so we acknowledge that, um, there's, there are a number of, of, of women in, in rough sleeping situations. We also acknowledge the number of, um, its disproportionate impact, um, on Maori as well. Um, I don't have the breakdown with me, uh, specifically around, uh, uh, women, but what I can say is just as I said to the member before, um, we... Well, if I take our initiatives that we had, we talked about when we launched in September.

Um, um, we, uh, the ministers agreed to our advice, which was to provide for 300 additional places because we knew that from talking to, um, Housing First providers that there was at least, um, 700 across Auckland, Hamilton, Wellington, and Christchurch of their clients that weren't housed. So they're providing support service to them, but they couldn't provide a house. We also knew that from discussions with them, there was roughly around 400, 423 of that s- 700 that were actually rough sleeping. From that, we know there will be a,

a, a, a make-up of, um, disproportionate number of Maori, there'll be single men in there. There will also be, also be, uh, women as well. Um, we have also, what we also did then was made sure that actually, we would fund 300 new places and we would also work with the providers who are increasing capacity for another 100 and so places that would meant we could tackle that 423 places that we were told by home- Housing First providers in those four areas. By tackling those th- that- by providing for that, um, supply, I have no doubt

that we'll make a dent in terms of, um, the number of women who are on the street living rough. And, um, just as, can I ask a quick sub on that? Um, just in terms of those Housing First places, how are you generally finding the provision of Housing First in Auckland compared to Wellington? Um, so I think in all... So at the moment, we're making a bit more headway, I'd say, in Auckland.

Mm-hmm. However, I, uh, I don't think that's an issue with any provider, um, and I don't think it's an issue, uh, uh... I mean, 'cause at the end of the day, the Housing First providers are working with CHPs. So if I take the Wellington example, Ds- uh, DCM is working with, um- Dwell.

... Dwell, yep. Um, and I know that they've made a good headway and they've got opportunities in the pipeline. We hope to see that's come to fruition pretty quickly. Um, in Auckland, there's an, a wider num- uh, range of providers- Mm-hmm.... uh, from, um, Te Kahu, uh, to, um, Lifewise, to- Vision West.

... Vision West. Um, and so there's a bit more headway being made in, in Auckland. But both markets, um, or both areas, you know, we- they are going to the private market and asking for landlords to provide their properties. And that is always something that, you know, is a, is a challenge. These are clients who have complex needs, um, and not all landlords, you know, would want to obviously, um, necessarily s- uh, work with the Housing First providers. Uh, my view, s- prejudice on this, is I'd encourage any private

landlord, if they're approached by a Housing First provider, to really consider, um, working with, uh, the CHP and working with the Housing First provider, 'cause this is a really, um, important matter, and we wanna get these people into more stable and secure housing. Supplementary, Hillian Taylor. Okay. So- Mm-hmm. ... I've tried to ask this question of K.O.

a few times, and they've told me you are the people, right? And it fits with this issue over women, because I went to the Women's Shelters Conference, and there was a whole lot of data from, um, Canada about, um, women not being... They, they're basically turned away by the refuges if there are domestic violence situations.

Um, 70% of women without children are turned away and they never come back because they go back into those situations, or they go on the street. So we have also women not being able to buy houses, um, who are single and older, so we have a whole kind of precariousness about w- women. My question is this. How much of this information, that kind of prediction of where that's, that need is, are you gathering, and do you see yourselves as the lead that's gonna tell K.O.

what to build, where to build it- Y- yeah.... for groups like this, cohorts like this? How aware of it are you, and how predictive is it? Okay, so, uh, that is-So, I would say in the past, we haven't, uh, been as, uh, that's hard, um, and also, um, other parties we've worked with have not used the data that has been available. Um, and I don't think we've understood clearly enough the cohorts that we, uh, tried to help.

Um, we are very much focused, and this is the GPS hub, on helping those in h- in, in high housing need, and I don't think, as I said, we've had the right data and the right, um, uh, setup to be able to actually respond. Um, now, what I would say on that is, um... I'll let you talk about the, the Māori stuff soon, um, which is important, um, is that within HUD, we've done a number of things over the last cou- couple of years and more recently. So, one is we've really upped our data, our data area, um, to be a lot clearer

on, um, what impact we're having, which cohorts, uh, we are supporting and, and what the impacts might be. Now that mat- that, that work over the last two years has enabled us to actually support the Minister in the release of his investment plan last year, and that's critical in terms of helping KO know where to build in the future, once they're able to build net new again. Equally, where they should be renewing their stock. So, you know, we want... KO's going to have to renew amount- a large amount of stock over the future years.

We want to be clear on where, which is the best place for that stock to be renewed. Do you just renew 500 in Auckland, um, and put 500 back there? Or do you renew 400 in Auckland and put 50 somewhere else and 20 somewhere else and make up the difference? So, getting to the how do we do that, that's why we've got the investment plan that was released and the Minister sort of addre- talked about that at a high level, and I'm really happy if you want me to go into more detail about how we've worked through that investment plan, the kind of data and how that will guide our

investments in the future. I should say the investment plan at the moment is g- is focused on how we, uh, use the flexible fund that was announced in Budget '25. The investment plan should not be seen as just a point in time for the flexible fund. If there are future funds in the few- uh, uh, uh, you know, in future years and there are few- different needs based on the government of the day, this investment plan now provides us an ability to be quite focused on w- what we want to invest in, where we want to invest.

And as the Minister said, you know, right homes, the right place for the right people. Do you want me to talk about... is it useful if I go into the investment plan? Okay. Well, so I might get... Do you want to come up? Adam? Yeah. While Adam's coming up, uh, one of the other things I wanted to add is we've made a shift in the organization to work in a place-based way.

So we're able to focus in on communities and understand what the communities need in each of those particular places, which will help inform some of the work that we're doing around the investment plan. Before you answer it, can I just give you another context which I'm really interested in? Mm-hmm. I'm, I'm the MP for Mount Albert. It's getting a whole lot of intensification and it's got a population that's aging.

So I'm very interested in aspects of this plan that might enlighten me as to how those two things go together when we're creating new zoning plans and we're going to have affordable housing. It must be really hard to predict what you're going to need. But how are you going about taking that place into account?

Yeah. And also, you've got an aging population that are no longer able to afford a house, so they're, they're going to retire without a house. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. So I'll, I'll have... I'll answer it while everyone... and you can... you'll come in with probably a bit more detail. So, so, um, first of all, so, so key part of the...

a key part of the investment plan is about saying, okay, right, if the objective is high, high housing need, l- where are the highest... where, where are the places in New Zealand with the highest housing need, right? So we've... and, and how do you distribute the flexible fund, which is basically social housing and affordable rental? So, we start with... there's a bit of a process we go through. We start with looking at, um, the housing register. We start looking... and we also look at severe housing deprivation, which

is coming off the census. That takes you so far and it gives you different results because as the Minister said, far north, you can look at the housing register and go, "Hmm, doesn't look too much of an issue." Severe housing deprivation and you go, "Oh my God, we have a big... we have a bigger issue here than we'd realized." So that's the first point, of course. What we also then do is we go, okay, what's happening in that market? What's the market growth expected? What's the housing growth?

What's going to be the demand? What's already happening in the market around pricing and other factors? Um, we also, uh, talk and check, check, okay, what do we know around the data and the cohorts and who might need social housing or affordable rental in that, in that area? We then have also checked that with the communities. We're spending quite a bit of time going out because the data tells you something, so- so it gives you a start and might be the answer, but it's not always the, the complete picture.

Mm-hmm. Then what we have done, um, is go, okay, what is the level of growth that housing that is already expected over future years and, and you know, forecasting what... again, it's population growth and other demands and how much will that we think address, you know, what's on the housing register and severe housing deprivation? Will it address it enough where we go, yep, not acknowled- not discounting there might be a need, but we...

may be better to invest in some other place in New Zealand still within the money we have available? What we then do is, um, go, okay, we also then the last check is how much actual government housing has already been invested in that area? So if you, you look at the investment plan, uh, that's been released, you'll notice that Rotorua isn't on the list. Now that's not because Rotorua is in a high housing need and in fact Rotorua there's, coming off the sub, there's over 500 social homes and m- and affordable rentals to be built.But we looked at

Rotorua and we said, based on the stats, based on the level of government investment, actually we can invest our money somewhere else in a higher need place. And that was because of the amount, ultimately because of the amount of government investment that was already planned to go in there over the next two years. So, that's, that's the process we went through.

Um, do you want to say anything else to that? I think you've given quite a, quite a full answer there Brad. What I would say is, um, a- as Brad said, the investment plan is around government's investment in social and affordable housing from a, what is government going to subsidize, uh, for people, on the housing register or in similar positions.

I think one of the responses to your question, too, is we need to actually look at the market as a whole and so this is part of the government's going for housing growth program, which is actually understanding in different areas, actually what is, what are the number of houses that are going to be needed in those areas over time, and setting targets, uh, to be able to get councils to do that. So, so I think there's both a piece here which is about government investment- Yeah.

... but there's also a piece here about actually what's that regulatory environment that the government's establishing to ensure that housing supply continues to flow regardless of the, the government investment side of it as being only one part. Can I ask a quick question on that? Just, um, what are some examples of some incentives that you think might work for local government to get building and doing their part?

I think, um, so look, I think one of the biggest, uh, changes is, is what the minister has talked about for going for housing pr- uh, plans, so um, to go for housing growth program which is two things. One, how do you get actually land supply, more land supply so it can be developed and how do you have, how do you get that quicker and on scale?

And the second is, how do you make sure that you get a, a development or a funding and financing model for infrastructure that is more enabling? So, um, if I take the councils and discussion, uh, and discussions I have with a number of councils, uh, common, uh, and, and also with developers and other parties including community housing providers, um, who are building in place in KO is- Sorry.

... restrictive planning rules are killing us. We cannot get enough land, uh, available. Um, second is, uh, infrastructure funding and financing is slow because, um, there's not clear about who's paying for it. Um, and a lot of the time under the current regime with development contributions, the actual cost goes back on the rate payer rather than necessarily the beneficiary.

Mm. Thank you, Ciaran. Thank you. Um, earlier today Minister Poor Tucker indicated that in his view, um, some regions have seen an increase in homelessness and some regions have seen a decrease in homelessness. Presumably, that view is based on advice that you've passed on. Can you inform the committee of the areas that have seen a drop in homelessness?

Um, so minister is right. Uh, but I think this is, uh, a... Some areas will always only expect we'll always see a, a, an increase and some will stay stable and some will drop. Um, as I said, um, before, I'm not gonna get ahead of the release of the insights report.

Um, what I am- With respect, I haven't asked you to do that. I'm not, I'm not asking what's in the report and in fairness the report doesn't cover these areas in great detail. The frontline providers or councils you give maybe three or four. There are- Mm.... many more than that. And, um, and I'm not asking you to divulge what information you've provided in the report.

Okay. But when... And, and with respect, when a minister makes a claim like that based on advice that you've provided, that is a legitimate question that deserves a response. Okay. So, um, I think, uh, so from I think the best way to respond to that is, is, is to wait for the insights report to come out, um, because, um, there are a range of, uh, there is more, uh, there will be the regions, uh, that will be highlighted in that report.

And it will be clear as to also, uh, clear as to also the sources of, um, data as well. Because as I said before, the ranges of the, the data sources can range from counts, to lists, to reports from the public. Um, and different forms of data you can have more confidence about than others.

Um, so, uh, respectfully I'd rather, uh, I'd rather, uh, I think the best, best response from, uh, I can give you is that the insights report will be out soon. Uh, that, uh, will have the latest information in it. Um, and, um, yeah. New question, Maureen?

Uh, thank you. Mine actually follows on- Oh, great.... a little bit from, um, some of the responses you gave just before which was around how you forecast building and where you forecast it to be. Mm-hmm. Um, but built into there is also the affordability aspect in home ownership. Mm. So, so, um, and I asked the minister this, this morning too, and he said to run it past you guys.

(laughs) Okay. Um, is, so when you're thinking about the planning, uh, for investment into the long term, so we all know what the immediate need is- Mm.... but very long term, what are you looking at in terms of where the direction of travel may head? Mm. And, um, and at what point do we reach saturation point?

So, I think if you go back to the going for housing growth, first of all, I don't think it's, uh, talk about there is, uh, settings from Wellington around what the growth level should be in, in particular places. What going for housing growth is about, and what the, um, uh, the changes that are proposed and the, uh, and the RM reforms are, are intended to do which is to-... put the, put the, uh... Ensure that local councils, local communities, um, can, uh, clear- clearly ascertain what their 30-year housing needs are. Um, and, uh, that will be prescribed

in- in- in legislation, um, and then backed up through secondary legislation as well. Um, so as part of that process, uh, what you would expect is that, um, local bodies, uh, would be working to work out exactly what is the development capacity needed in 30 years based on the best information that they have, which will be a measure of obviously population growth, uh, developable land and so on. Um, that is the best way and if you... Uh, that is the- and ultimately that's about unlocking land supply, um, because I think it's a recognition from

advice from HUD and also from the minister that the best way we can actually address affordability in this country is actually to build more houses. Um, yes that is never gonna... That's there are some people who are gonna still need housing support and we've just talked about that, but ultimately for the majority of New Zealanders who want more affordable housing- (clears throat)... it is getting more supply out there.

And so by- by- by providing for mechanisms, uh, through the RM changes that councils have to build, uh, have capacity for 30 years and supporting that through some other changes to remove restrictive planning rules, to remove silly things like, um, you know, what the size of your balcony can be or- or- or the ability for councils to say your planter box has to be red or green.

Um, or the size of your cycle, um, uh, park. Um, that is all about removing restrictions, getting land available, getting land, getting houses built as quickly as possible and that is h- getting supply, and that is how we will get affordability improved in the country. Can I ask something on that?

Yeah, sure. What about tax? Uh... Oh that's- that's really, that's not a matter that we have, um, advised ministers about. Um, I don't have a... I don't, we don't have a very clear view on- on- on tax. Um, that's a matter that's being worked through with any tax matters we work through with Treasury and IRD.

I'll, I'll ask probably a couple of subs on this actually. I was going to make it a separate question, but seeing as it's gone there anyway. (laughs) Yeah. Um, so you said in your initial comments that you're providing advice on, um, these regional deals, uh, and around the country. Mm-hmm. And- and I've got a historic interest in one of the Otago Central Lakes one. Right. So I'm not going to ask you to- to- to delve into that too deeply.

Okay. But what I'm interested in is sort of backward-looking, in terms of the data that you have, and you mentioned earlier in response to a different question, that there's some issues around data historically. Um, (clears throat) and I know, I know in Queenstown Lakes, for example, that, um, some data, uh, there's- these- these are a whole variety of different data sources they're relied on. Statistics New Zealand, the, uh, district councils have their own data sets.

Mm-hmm. Um, and the Statistics Commission has their own data sets. Mm-hmm. Uh, what we do know is that consistently for decades, um, that the prediction about future growth has been significantly undercooked by everyone. Um, and that's, I think, made quite real challenges for our region. We're- we're one example of a number across the country.

Mm-hmm. Really, we are thinking because there hasn't been adequate data and adequate projections before dashboarded to decision-makers, there hasn't been adequate decision-making about what the future development is going to be. Housing, schooling, health services- (clears throat)... roading, electricity generation, you know, supply, et cetera. Yeah. So I'm- I'm interested in what work you're doing, or your department's doing, to really critique those historic data sets, match them to actual growth predictions, and sometimes declines, depends on which part of the

country, to- to try and make sure the dashboard, both with the new organization and to other decision-makers, the best set of predictions possible, you know, given- given that... Yeah. So, um,............................ back. Our

focus around data has been more around forward-looking. Um, you know, how do we get better understanding going forward around, you know, um, uh, yeah, um, you know, what might be the, uh, well particularly at regional level, what might be the level of market housing growth. And ultimately, as I said before- Mm-hmm.

... uh, that is gonna be, uh, something that is gonna be required, uh, from local councils as part of, uh, you know, communities as part of the- the RM changes. Um, we rely a lot currently on stats data. Um, and, um, and that gives us, has given us a sense in the past and we use that obviously for, um, for the investment plan around, you know, population levels and severe housing deprivation and so on.

Um, we, the way we are dealing with that now though, too, is around the investment plan is actually going out there to make sure we engage in a lot more in depth with communities because we're not getting this... You don't always get the full picture from- from- from stats data or just from the data you- you gather yourselves as an entity. Um, what I'd, what I think is more important in terms of the way to answer your question is, is what we do going forward.

So with going for, with going for housing growth, um, and with the, um, Planning Act changes, um, and reforms that will be, um, going through the House, we need to be, we- we will want to be really clear about are- are- are these changes, are these really key settings actually making a tangible difference? And that is the focus of a lot of our work now which is, okay, how do we measure the impact those changes are gonna have?... um, how do we, how do we...

And a lot of that will still be required on st- uh, will s- still be dependent on stats data, but that's the focus at the moment. Um, do you want to say anything else, uh, Rachel, on that? I think it's a good point, that we rely on statistics (indistinct) Yeah. They do go Yup. But can I Can, can I, can I, Yes.

... just sugg- um, encourage you to challenge it? Uh, because if you look back at last 20 years of data for, say, Queensland Lakes, when I... Just an example that I'm familiar with- Mm-hmm.... district. Uh, if you smooth it over 20 years, you're looking at 5.4% annual average growth. Right. Um, compounding. Yeah. Whereas that, if you look at the projections currently, it's a bit...

little over 2% growth. And that's what a lot of d- departments are making their decisions based on. Mm-hmm. Uh, and, you know, so if you look at history- Mm-hmm.... and you look, you look at pro- predictions that were made at the time with best information available- Yeah.... it doesn't match up. Yeah. Um, so can I, can I, um- No, that's important.... en- encou- encourage you to- Yup. ... to stress test- Yup.

... those assumptions- Yup.... and to make sure those future predictions are, are good ones. Yeah. But we're always looking to improve this. I think whether... As I said before, um, around the investment plan, that is a, that is quite a, that's a, a sort of big step forward in how we use data to be much more informed now about where do we actually want to invest to make the biggest impact.

And in that area, we have, um, since checked it with some of the communities as well. Right, right. Add a bit of rigor to our data. All right. Take your point. Um, New Question Time, I Hey, um, keen to talk about renting stuff. Um, so, the... You guys put out a report in the last few weeks, I believe, and it talked about Healthy Homes compliance. Um, obviously, it was pre-June, so before the latest round of, um, compliance rules came in on the 1st of July. Um, in it, in it, the, the really striking statistics for, for me were that 59%

of rental properties do not have under-floor insulation, and that half of renters say that their home is damp, or moldy, or difficult to heat in winter. Um, what are some things that MHUD could potentially look at, or the government could look at to increase compliance for Healthy Homes?

Okay. I'll let Claire... Claire's in charge of the- Oh, cool. Awesome. Yeah. Um, so the reason we did that report just before, just before the 1 July deadline was to try and get a sense of how we were tracking towards, um, landlords' compliance. Mm-hmm. So, um, what we did see was that 90% of landlords were saying that... It is self-reported, but they are saying that they were on track to comply or, or would have completed that by 1 July.

Mm-hmm. So, that's a very positive sign. Um, and the, the things were, their engagement was moving in the right direction quite substantially since the report prior. So, um, I think that's really positive. Um, I don't have the under-floor insulation, uh, information in front of me, but I know there are quite a few properties that there's an exemption for. So, um, so for example, if there's an apartment- Yeah.

... then there's, then you're not required to have that. And if there's a, a more modern, um, construction style is that you've got a concrete pad. So, quite often, the... If it's a new build, it might have insulation within the pad, but then the resident themselves won't be able to check that it's not... It's not kind of the same as if you've got a property that's lifted up off the ground and you can pop your head under and check.

Um, so around that, that there can be inter- some interpretation, um, issues whe- when, if it's the tenant that's self-reporting. Mm-hmm. And there's a re- and there's a reasonable number of scenarios where that's allowed. The key thing with Healthy Homes was, is that we were requiring what, what the physical things are that can be done with the building. So, that's making sure that the property, um, you know, the property has the device there to heat the room, that there is the ability to ventilate.

And so, that's providing the physical changes to the property that are then, then the, the residents themselves will need to work through what, to what level they can afford to heat. And so, the electricity prices and what they're able to claim to support around, um, any additional income supplement to cover that cost, that all plays into whether there is dampness and whether they can afford to heat the home.

But what we've, what we have done is provide the tools there for them to use once they've worked through accessing other support so that they can afford to heat their home. We've also got, um, a lot of, uh, support around advice and guidance about they... how they could choose to ventilate differently, how you can choose to use your home differently up on the MBIE website, um, to support people to think about when they might ventilate, for example, where they might put furious things, uh, to try to minimize that.

And is it, is it too soon, since those new, um, rules came in on the 1st of July to kind of... Like, are you seeing any trends around, um, tenants being a bit more confident to enforce those standards, or are there... Can you pick up any kind of early trends? We are seeing that there are, um...

We're seeing positive signs around applications to the Tenancy Tribunal, even like, in the... You know, we've been watching that in the years before, um, before full compliance was required, that people are taking cases. Uh, and, and we're seeing that that is a theme as well post, um, 1 July. But I think there's, there's also, um, some market forces stuff going on there with...

In some markets like Wellington, there being, um, more properties on the market, uh, tenants can also ch- choose that, the ability they have with a bit more choice availability, a bit more negotiability on the rent to actually choose when... choose between properties that are compliant when they're, when they're making those choices about where to move to next.

Mm-hmm. So- And, um, yeah, I'm moving into a new flat next week. (laughs) Okay.Uh, just also wanted to ask, have you seen any trends from, um, no-cause evictions were brought back in, and I think may... came to force in the beginning of the year. Have you seen any trends from that law change?

So we have been... Um, when I've been meeting with the Tenancy Advocates Network and meeting with, um, property investors, um, both at the Property Investors Conference and the Tenancy Advocates Network, we've asked those questions about what, you know... so when are landlords using those, um, no-cause evictions, and what were the tenancy advocates, um, hearing?

Mm-hmm. They weren't... The last time I spoke to them, they weren't really seeing a trend of increased use, and that different dynamic in the private market, I think, is playing into that because landlords are... They've obviously got to think about their continuity around their rental income, and so they're thinking very carefully about when they're gonna use that notice. That was the theme I was hearing, both from landlords and also that... when I was asking tenant advocates, like, "Do you have examples of it being

used? Is it being used in the right place?" That... They weren't... There weren't examples at that time that they could give that they were particularly concerned about. And I think it's the mark- the market being where it is, um, that, that is perhaps playing through with it as well, and that the tenants have more choice, and the landlords are weighing up, um, when they're, when they're using that.

Your question, Hill? I wanna ask about the Carrington development- Mm-hmm.... which, um, the first thing is they've ****** to the, um, buildings that have gone up. They're really, really lovely buildings. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. Um, I understand that they were built with an underwrite from the government. Um, is that within your purview?

Yep, yep. So one, one of them, one of them- One of them was.... Ockham apartment was, yep. Sounds like that mean it got built. Is that a tool in the toolbox that you intend to use again given the results are so good? So, um, so the RDU, uh, uh, tool...

Just step back. So that was, uh, that was launched October last... October last year? Yeah. (laughs) That's right. Um, so it was intended to do, uh, uh, two things. One was to help maintain a certain level of construction activity in, in the country. Um, and we saw the, uh, issues with that not happening after the, uh...

around the GFC time where we lost a lot of, uh, construction capacity. But kept the pipeline going. What's that? Kept the pipeline going. The second was obviously to, uh, provide for housing supply. Um, so, um, uh, we've had... We've got about, I think, nine and maybe... if... I may have signed another one yest- uh, I think it's an- another one yesterday.

Uh, underwrites that we've so far proc- uh, com- uh, a- approved, contracted. Uh, the Ockham one being one of them. Um, we've got a certain, uh, uh, of, uh, envelope of money. Uh, that's basically a... think of it as a risk contingency. And it's to how much would the government be up for if all the, the underwrites were called in to a certain level.

Um, I think the program is... has been, uh, very well received by not just the... you know, by the residential construction sector. It's actually in a number of places. We've had... We've under... We've, um... Deals have been done in Auckland, uh, Wellington, uh, Hamilton, Queenstown. Um, in fact, two recently in Queenstown. Um, so I think, uh, it is definitely, um, meeting its objective.

Um, in terms of, uh, the Carrington site, um, if there are, uh, proposals, um, for that site from developers who want to access the RDU, then we will actively look at them. Um, but what we do with each, each proposal is we've got to weigh up a couple of things. One is, ultimately, um, are we underwriting the sales risk, uh, which is key. Um, and we have to w- and obviously what kind of housing supply is occurring.

We have to factor that or balance that against what's the risk to the Crown. So we always look at, for example, what is the demand in a place? What is the other sales that are happening in a place? Is there... As a member talked about, is there saturation and too many properties on the market there, so does the Crown risk get elevated as a result?

So there's all those factors we weigh up. But, um, uh, very... Uh, we've got a number in the pipeline, a number of other, uh, deals, and, um, processing those. Can I ask you now, this is the non-****. That site has a whole lot of different iwi building, and when I tried to hold a public meeting- Mm-hmm.

... at, I couldn't get anyone to talk to me about what was going on there, and it's really hard for the community. R- okay. Um, when, um, we found out the other day that Penman House was going, there was a lot of emotion around it. To be fair, I'd been talking to MHUD, nobody had mentioned it. What's going on with communication at MHUD?

'Cause it's a hard job you've got. Mm-hmm. So we- Are you telling communities enough to keep their buy-in? Diora. Um, so the, the great news is that we are handing that site officially back over to the three rōpū who, who, um, are, uh... who's, uh, who will continue to develop that site.

So HUD has, has... We have almost completed our job of, of acquiring the land. Uh, we're now in development agreements with, um, the three rōpū bar one, one small agreement. Um, but we're now in a great position where, um, we stand behind those three rōpū, and they are now, um, in full control as they, as they have been. They are the developers on that land.

So we can support further conversations, but we would, um... but that would be-... with, with them. That's pretty much the answer that I've got, a long time before now, that it's nothing to do with MHUD, I've got to go to the iwi. But you are the project managers for that site, and you're the link to the community.

Ag- again, we would do everything with the ropu's- Okay. ... um, s- standing beside them a- as the, as their partners on this, but they are, they are the developers of that land. I think to be, to be, just to be clear, our, our role around, this is a for, this is a land for housing or Crown surplus land, um, acquisition. Um, so the site became surplus to requirements, we acquired it, acquired two parcels over the last sort of five, six, seven years.

2018. Since 2018. Um, what we do is, and there's obviously RFRs, we've worked with the three ropu, um, and what, uh, Jessica's talking about is our role is to, is to get the development agreements in place, which is basically when are you gonna build, how much are you gonna build, what yield are you gonna build, and by the way, how quickly can we then get the capital returned back to the, the Crown?

And, but you get, you ropu, you carry the development risk. And so when we're asked to sort of comment about what the plan is or project managing, actually the ropu are the actual ex, uh, are the, uh, are responsible for that, and they will be working with their development partners as well, whether it be Ockham or Fletchers or anyone else. Um, so we, we don't get involved in then managing the ongoing development. That is the ropu.

New question, Maureen. Uh, thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, I've got a question around transitional housing. Okay. Um, so just, (clears throat) just wondering if you've got the figures there on the vacancy rate- Oh.... of transitional housing. And, um, depending on what the answer is, um, what is the plan going forward?

I can answer the occupancy rate, if that helps. Oh, that's great. Yes. Um, so at the moment it's just over 90% across the motu. We have around, around 6,000 places around, um, the country. So, um, through our, our, uh, ongoing, uh, BAU utilization of, of transitional housing, we're working with those providers to see what tweaks we can do and working with them to ensure that we have the, the least days possible between seeing one client, uh, exit and another client come in.

Um, we're also looking at, um, with particular providers why some, some particular clients might have stayed in there longer, but the average is about 20 weeks- Right.... um, for, for each person to stay in transitional housing. So yeah, we're, we're reasonably happy with where the full portfolio is. We would love to see, um, further improvements, but they will be tweaks around the edges as there's the, there, as it is a complex thing to be able to match the right person, um, at the right time with the right provider. Each provider has different cohorts that they look

after. Might be wahine, might be, um, uh, single men. So it's not, it's not always, uh, 100% occupancy is, is, um- Idealistic?... not a- No.... not utopian too, and not, yeah, not a utopia we could ever expect out of that particular- Mm-hmm. Um, so just another question around, um- Mm-hmm.

... the, the length of time between occupancies. Yes, yeah. So, so what is the type of thing that you do there and how long is- Yeah.... that usually vacant? So at the moment it's around 14 calendar days in between one client exiting and one client coming in. The whole b- being transitional housing, we see as much, almost as much inflow as we do outflow. It's around about 4,000 each month coming in, 4,000 each month going, going out.

And we know that when they do exit transitional housing, um, around 60% do go into a, a form of long-term housing, be it social housing or, or market. Um, and another almost 20% or so, um, into another form of housing to another provider or back to whānau or, or, or, or another situation.

Right. Um, and the other question is, um, are you noticing a significant drop in the number of children in transitional housing given that they've got priority now, um, to come out of emergency housing into more permanent housing? Yeah, we, we have seen recently a, a, a change in cohort. Um- Wow.

Yes. (laughs) Okay. Um, yes, we have seen- So it's working.... a small drop in, in children. Thank you. Supplementary, lieutenant. Um, when the minister, when, sorry, Minister Potaka came in and was talking to us about, uh, youth transitional housing, he mentioned a new contract signed with Matihuruhuru for youth transitional. Yeah. That was actually around our near-term actions where we're working with Matihuruhuru on, on youth outreach.

Oh. Yeah. Yeah. So yep- Yeah. ... he, he was referring to that specific- Mm-hmm. So-... contract, but we do have transitional housing with them............................ Oh, so that contract doesn't sit with you guys, the youth outreach? The youth outreach does. It's part of our near-term actions to address homelessness, um, part of the 10, 10 million that was, uh, announced by ministers in September.

Oh, yeah. So... So they'll, they'll go out, talk to homeless young people- Correct.... and then .............................. And connect them and connect them back in-... to their services. ... to the most appropriate, um- Really nice. Um, Manaaki Rangatahi, uh, you, you'll probably be aware of are just upstairs launching their, um, More Than a Home report. Um, and I met with them last week because I knew I was gonna be in here. And one of the big points they raised is their concern that there's no youth transitional housing in Rotorua, Napier,

Taupō, Gisborne, Kaiāpuhe, Whangarei, and the North Shore. Um, I know it's not up to you how much money you get allocated for youth transitional housing, but what do you understand around the need in those more provincial areas, and how can we put those providers in the best position to be able to be doing the same things as Matihuruhuru?

We do have, um, we have Rangatahi-supported housing at the moment, 50 places. That's, that's incredibly intense provision, 24-hour, um, uh, wraparound service. We've also got our, our specific youth transitional housing with some of those providers.

We also, uh, within our, um, within our broader 6,000 places, there are some youth places in that we just haven't, uh, uh, called it youth transitional housing. So indeed, yeah, that there is, there is need. There's also other, um, other services and support services that are available for, for- I-... the youth cohort as well.

I would say, just in addition, so we can look at the provision of transitional housing to support, you know, young people in need. But, um, you know, there's the 1,500 allocated in Budget '24 that'll obviously, you know, be providing stable and secure housing, and that's social housing rather than transitional. Um, if you look at the, uh, the investment plan that was announced two weeks ago, it is going into areas where, quite frankly, uh, the level of investment has not been, um, suitable in the past. And if I take these,

places like, uh, Opotiki, um, uh, Whakatane, Far North, you know, places where, quite frankly, um, there ha- there should, there w- the need is the highest and we haven't made the investment, that will have a disproportionate, um, impact obviously on, on, on youth, particularly in the Far North there's high, high youth population.

Um, there is in those places too, I think it's important to remember, there is still housing support products, whether it be through HUD or through MSD. You know, if I think of, um, you know, Rent to Rez and Bond which is there to help, you know, obviously including youth to, to stay in their existing housing situation rather than fall into a homeless situation as well.

Mm-hmm. And just- New question, Kieran. Oh, can I ask a quick sub on that? We, we have to move on. There's a few people waiting. Thank you. Um, this morning when asked about the 1,500 places funded in Budget '24, the minister said, um, quote, "Yes, I'm advised that it is on track." Uh, your aide memoire to the minister on the 28th of August, uh, demonstrates that isn't the case. The original timeframe, uh, was 1,500 to be delivered by June '27, and the Annex A, Risk to 1,500 Delivery, it indicates that, uh, we've got 337 in financial year

'25/'26 and another 147 before June, uh, '27. That falls short. Do you have an update on these numbers? This was at- So-... 31st of July. So, um, yes, I do. So, uh, first point, um, we're very much focused on continuing to target delivery by, um, on the, on the Budget '24 places, 1,500, by, um, end of June 2027. Um, I think what you were referring to, I think, um, there might be a s- slight misinterpretation in terms of the information, uh, or your, uh, the information, um, that you're

reading. Um, so first of all, the briefing, uh, received, uh, from officials for Minister Bishop several months ago and reflected progress on Budget '24 at the end of July, it didn't fire, it wasn't intended to provide a full picture of planned delivery. And- And with, with respect, that's why I'm asking for an update now.

So I'm giving you, yep. Yeah, I'm getting there. Yep. So the table in Annex A that you referred to show the expected numbers of places that will be delivered in each year and quarter, and this is based on the Ministry's understanding as at 31 July. I think what has happened here is, um, you've identified, um, because we've gone back and looked at this, the Q3 and Q4 delivery numbers for '26/'27 has been attributed to the following financial year, in other words the calendar year, instead of actually within the budget year.

So when we referred in our report to Q- Q3/Q4, we are referring up until end of June '27, which is exactly the target date. Um, so- Okay. Well, I, I'm sorry to jump in but we've got six minutes and, and pending questions so- So can I- Oh. Even if that is the case, you've got 989 as the sum total there.

So that was, as I said, that was as of July. Yeah, so where are we now? As of July and as of, as the minister talked about, uh, previously, as of 31 October 2025, of the 1,500, we have got 62 in proposed or under evaluation, we've got 1,326 approved, um, and they will be moving forward with construction, and we've got 115 delivered to date. And I would say also that, um, that, you know, we would expect that delivered number to be closer to 200, uh, by the end of this year as well.

So 1,358 have been allocated out of that 1,500? So of the 1,500, 62 are still under evaluation, 1,326 have been approved- 16.... and, and 115 have been delivered. So 1,388. Okay, and how many of those, uh, have been allocated to private developers on a build-to-lease basis?

Well, so, so they, so the deals we do are always through community housing providers. Uh, we, but through, uh, this allocation, uh, working with community housing providers, um, we, uh, and I think the minister had, uh, acknowledged we're gonna be a- more agnostic. We've said to community housing providers, "What matters here is getting these places built in the timeframe."... good value for money in the places that we have prioritized. So, from that perspective, we said to the CHPs, "You can go out there and you might wanna do build to own.

You might also want to do build to lease." But at the end of the day, community housing providers don't build, developers build. Now, at the end of the d- but going back, our deals are still with the CHPs. The CHPs still have to provide for that place while the funding goes through the CHPs, and they have to manage the tenancy.

So, how many are in the lease arrangements rather than own? I think I ... Have you got that, Sir? Yeah. So, I don't, I don't have the precise numbers of lease, but, uh, it is less than 50%, uh- Okay.... build to lease. Would you mind following up with the committee w- once you can go back and find those numbers, please? Oh, there's that num- yeah, sure. Fine. Thank you. It would be useful.

Uh, Nicholas Umpolo. Um, my question has to do with the strategic financial management of the ministry, um, and, um, how you're doing with assessing value for money, uh, and also, the effectiveness of housing initiatives. Um, do you, are you seeing progress in this?

Is it in informing your long-term planning? Yeah. Um, do you need additional information or capability to get there? So, um, I'm really impressed with the work we've done around value for money, um, and I've seen the, see the benefits of that come through in Budget '24 and Budget '25. And we're continuing to, everything we do, we're continuing to look from a value for money lens because ultimately, there's a lot of money, as I said before in my introduction, you know, eight, eight, 8 billion go per annum coming through

HUD. We ... That's a lot of money, a lot of taxpayers' money, and we want to make sure it's having an impact. And particularly an impact for those who have the highest housing need across the country. So, in Budget '24, just as a, a case in point, uh, value for money, we, we, um, assessed a number of programs. Some weren't having the impact we wanted.

Some were due to finish anyway, so there wasn't additional funding ongoing for them. But in Budget '24, we were able to fi- uh, to, uh, find savings, um, in the vicinity of net 1.5 billion, but through that process, we were able to, uh, fund additional 1,500 community housing, uh, social housing places. In Budget '25, I think our net sa- we found savings of close to 500 million, and from that they, we were able to fund, uh, significant, some significant cost pressures, including $100 million cost pressure over the forecast period for, um, for housing

first places that were already in existence, um, as well as, uh, finding 41 million plus 250 million capital over 10 years to fund the flexible fund, which is an additional 675 to 770 places. That's very good. I'm gonna take the opportunity to probably ask my last question too.

Okay. (laughs) Um, just that, uh, look, last, last time you were in front of the committee, we talked a little bit about, um, your leadership role in the overall housing and urban deve- development space. Yeah. And, uh, how you work with local government around the country, um, and there's, um, and there's, there's also the other piece which is, uh, you know, for example, working with the Ministry of Education around future schooling needs- Mm-hmm.

... and how that impacts on housing requirements and vice versa. Mm-hmm. Uh, transport, um, you know, our transport sector and NZTA, um, and, uh, regional councils in terms of delivery of public transport. Uh, and, you know, then you have, say, um, Transpower and different, you know, in terms of their delivery of the energy needed- Mm-hmm.

... et cetera, so, um, what levers do you have in this overall super leadership role? And how have they been working in the year in review? So, um, so we have a big focus on, on what you would call sort of urban growth, uh, we call it Urban Growth Partnerships.

Um, which is basically in some of our areas like Waikato, Auckland, um, Canterbury, your area, um, Wellington region, going okay, working with local councils, regional councils, you know, what is, what, what are development areas? You know, what are, where are we gonna build? Um, so if you ca- take the points in Auckland, City Rail and Drury, um, and City, uh, uh, and City Rail, uh, we've been working closely with Transport to go, okay, um, how do we make sure the sites around those, those stations are actually going to be developed?

Um, you get value and you get throughput through those stations the more people you have living around them, and that's the overseas, uh, experience and evidence. Um, so we're working to go with, with, um, Transport and also the Council to how do we unlock that land? Some of our land is, uh, I think one or two sites is kaitunga aura around one of the stations, but it's also, how do we actively work with the Council to make sure that they can get those sites developed, and they'll be working with private developers.

So, there's, uh, there's, uh, pretty much a, a nudging aspect coming from us to say, "Are you thinking about this, this how do we get the most benefits out of that, um, transport investment?" Um, in Drury, um, we are part of a developer group that is, uh, involves also other, um, other agencies like Health, like Education, like NZTA.

And what we're focused on there is, once again, okay, where are we gonna develop in Drury, over what timeframe, and what infrastructure needs to be provided? So, we play very much in a lot of the exam- two examples I've used, a coordination role. Because a lot of the time around infrastructure, funding and financing, getting that right, is absolutely critical. It's also getting your, getting that aligned and coordinated.

So, if you're gonna go and build a great big motorway in Tauranga and to Rico West, are you thinking at the same time around the land enablement and housing supply and how that's gonna come on? Because then ultimately, you can invest in, in transport and new corridors, but actually, how do you get people to live along them and use them as well? And that's where we are very much focused.

A lot of the time, as I said, our work is around, okay, have you thought about the housing supply? Have you thought about the land use? Have you thought about the intensification opportunities? Um, because the benefits accrue, additional benefits accrue, just from, for example, providing a road. Excellent. I'd, I'd like to have a lot more questions, but I think we have to..............................
Thank you. Thank you very much- No, thank you.... for coming before the committee and- Thanks.... thanks for the work that you're doing. Thank you. Okay. Cheers. Ta. Okay.
